Windsor chef Adelina Sisti-DeBlasis is among the elite members of Culinary Team Canada, who recently returned from the World Culinary Olympics with gold and silver medals.

Every four years the world’s best chefs don their aprons for the six-day competition in Stuttgart, Germany, where dreams of national pride and the perfect menu can rise and fall like an overbaked soufflé.

The medals are another feather in the chef’s toque for Sisti-DeBlasis, who was named National Chef of the Year in 2023 by the Culinary Federation of Canada.

This was her first trip to the Olympics of fine cuisine.

“The competition is fierce. It’s very intimidating at first,” said Sisti-DeBlasis, also a hospitality, tourism and co-op teacher for the GreaterEssex CountyDistrict School Board.

“So for my first, to bring home a gold is pretty exciting.”

The International Exhibition of Culinary Art, better known as the World Culinary Olympics, ran Feb. 2 to 7. It is the largest international culinary arts event on the planet.

Team Canada won gold in the Restaurant of Nations category, which required teams to prepare a three-course meal for 110 guests. They also took silver in the Chef’s Table category, preparing a seven-course menu for 14 people.  Overall, Canada ranked sixth in the world out of 24 teams.

Sisti-DeBlasis was part of the Canadian squad’s management team. Her jobs included communications, finance, administration, flavour critique, and recipe editor.

The vast Canuck-inspired menu included pine ice cream, Quebec First Nations spices, Newfoundland salt, sturgeon bacon, maple syrup, sea buckthorn berries, morel mushrooms, and bison.

“We used the boreal forest as our backdrop,” she said. “There were very unique flavours that were reminiscent of Canada.”

Overall, Finland won gold, Switzerland took silver, and the bronze went to Iceland. The national team from host country Germany finished behind Canada in ninth place.

“Sixth overall is very impressive because I believe we’re the only North American team to make the Top 10,” said Sisti-DeBlasis. “That feels fantastic.

“It’s been a decade or more for a national team to bring home a gold medal in Restaurant of Nations. It was very cool that that happened.”

Sisti-DeBlasis said the European teams that consistently take the podium have a major advantage the Canadians do not — government funding.

“It’s hard to get to the podium with them,” she said. “But this year we were extremely close. That speaks volumes that Canada is a force to be reckoned with.”

Unlike other countries, the Canadians have to do fundraisers and search for sponsors.

They also volunteer their time. And it’s a lot of time. The team members travelled from across Canada for five-day practice runs, usually in Toronto, every eight weeks for the last four years.

Sisti-DeBlasis said she was grateful for the “unwavering support” of her family.

“It’s not easy to be away so often,” she said. “They were so encouraging and excited to see the awesome results of this four-year commitment.”

For as long as Sisti-DeBlasis can remember, food has consumed her life.

“Food is sustainability,” she said. “We can’t survive without it. So it’s important for us to kind of step back and be thankful and grateful for all the fruits that are around us.

“Our lands are able to provide so much for us. As long as we treat it good, it will treat us better.”

Sisti-DeBlasis was only seven years old when she created her first cookbook. It included the groundbreaking recipe for Lemon Spree, her crisp and complex combination of lemonade, 7 Up, and a lemon twist.

“And just thinking I was a rockstar chef at seven years old.”

That passion was fostered by her late mother and father, Immacolata and Ferruccio Sisti.

“My mom was an excellent cook at the Ciociaro Club,” said Sisti-DeBlasis. “I loved watching her cook, sitting beside her and learning all the different techniques that she would do.

“I caught the bug from her. Subsequently, dad was a fantastic cook after my mom passed away. Just wanting to experiment and do different and unique things.”

She said her parents would have been “completely elated” to see how well Team Canada did.

“That was my first stop when I arrived back from Germany, I went to the cemetery to show them some cool hardware,” said Sisti-DeBlasis.

By bringing medals home to Windsor-Essex, and teaching young people to develop skills and dream big, she hopes to stir up the same passion in others that her parents inspired for her.

“I love teaching and I love cooking,” she said. “I think the more involved you are in your community, the more you’ll get out of whatever it is that you do.

“For me, it’s a no-brainer. Food is passion. Passion is food.

“Being able to get students excited about cooking, and maybe doing that for a living, is a win-win for everybody.”

twilhelm@postmedia.com

title: 'Fierce' — Windsor chef, Team Canada take World Culinary Olympics gold | Windsor Star
description: Windsor chef Adelina Sisti-DeBlasis was a member of Culinary Team Canada which won gold and silver medals at the World Culinary Olympics.
keywords: cooking, Culinary Olympics, Foods, Germany, News Videos, Team Canada, Windsor
